rationale: The cited paper is used as a concrete prior example in the citing paper's defensive-processing argument, not just as a background list citation. In the theory section, it is cited to support the claim that highly threatening health messages trigger message scrutiny and discounting. In the general discussion, the authors explicitly contrast their work with Agrawal and Duhachek's direct threat/emotion manipulations to position their own contribution as showing a different trigger of defensive responding.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Agrawal and Duhachek's defensive-processing findings in health persuasion and extends that line by showing that identity-salience cues can also produce defensive reactions, even without directly manipulating threatening message content. It uses the cited study as a key benchmark for what prior defensive-processing work had already established.
